title: Competition and Antitrust Law Enforcement Reform Act of 2025
short_title: Competition and Antitrust Law Enforcement Reform Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes it harder for big companies to merge together if it would hurt competition and make prices go up for customers. It stops companies from using unfair business practices to push out their competitors and lets the government fine companies that break these rules. The bill also creates new government offices to watch how companies behave in the market and protects workers who report when companies break competition laws. Finally, it gives more money to the agencies that enforce these laws so they can do their job better.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Competition and Antitrust Law Enforcement Reform Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings and Purposes
sections.text: Congress finds that competitive markets are essential for the American economy. When multiple companies compete to sell products and services, they must offer the best quality and choices at the lowest prices. Competition helps small businesses grow, reduces inequality, and creates jobs and innovation. It also helps America compete globally and protects national security. Today, too many companies have too much market power, which makes it harder for people to start new businesses, keeps wages low, and increases inequality. This especially hurts historically disadvantaged communities. Market power and concentration also leads to political power that harms democracy. When companies have monopoly power, they can charge higher prices, reduce quality and choices, block innovation, shut out competitors, and make it harder for new businesses to enter the market. When companies have monopsony power (control over buying), they can force suppliers and workers to accept lower prices and wages, which reduces quality, limits opportunities, and harms consumers and innovation. All types of mergers can create market power and harm competition. Since 2008, companies have merged or bought each other for over ten trillion dollars. When dominant companies buy potential competitors or disruptive companies that offer lower prices or new products, this threatens competition and innovation. Current court decisions and enforcement policies have weakened protections against harmful mergers by focusing too much on short-term price effects and ignoring other harms like reduced quality, less choice, and blocked innovation. Courts also require too much proof of harm and do not properly consider evidence of competitive damage. When dominant companies use exclusionary conduct to harm competitors, this seriously threatens the economy. Dominant sellers harm buyers by overcharging them and reducing quality and choices. Dominant buyers harm suppliers by underpaying them. Dominant employers harm workers by paying low wages and limiting opportunities. Courts have wrongly assumed that market power corrects itself and that monopolies drive innovation, which contradicts modern economic understanding. Courts have also improperly given companies immunity from antitrust laws based on federal regulations. Current civil remedies are not enough to stop anticompetitive conduct, and sometimes civil penalties are needed along with other remedies. Federal antitrust enforcement budgets have not kept up with the growing economy, which has weakened enforcement and contributed to rising market power. The purposes of this law are to strengthen antitrust enforcement by government agencies and private parties, make it easier to stop harmful mergers before they happen, clarify that mergers can be blocked for reducing quality or innovation in addition to raising prices, make clear that creating monopsony power violates the law, require parties to certain large mergers to prove they will not harm competition, prohibit exclusionary conduct that harms competition, allow civil penalties for Sherman Act violations, give enforcement agencies more resources and tools, protect and reward whistleblowers who report anticompetitive conduct, and give successful antitrust plaintiffs the right to prejudgment interest on damage awards.

sections.title: Section 3 Definition
sections.text: In this law, the term antitrust laws has the same meaning as in the Clayton Act. It also includes section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act when it applies to unfair methods of competition, and this law and its amendments.

sections.title: Section 4 Unlawful Acquisitions
sections.text: This section adds a definition of market power to the Clayton Act. Market power means the ability of a person or group of people working together to impose terms on others, including terms about price, quantity, quality, or other conditions that are more favorable than what they could get in a competitive market. This section changes section 7 of the Clayton Act to make it easier to block harmful mergers. It changes the standard from mergers that may substantially lessen competition to those that create an appreciable risk of materially lessening competition. It also adds that creating a monopsony (buyer monopoly) violates the law. The section creates specific rules for when courts should find that a merger may harm competition. These include when the merger would significantly increase market concentration, increase the ability to engage in exclusionary conduct, involve a company with more than 50 percent market share buying a competitor, combine competitors where one disrupts coordination among competitors, enable unilateral exercise of market power or increase coordinated interaction among competitors, or involve very large transactions. For the largest mergers involving companies with more than one hundred billion dollars in assets or market value, or mergers worth more than five billion dollars, the companies must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that the merger will not create an appreciable risk of materially lessening competition or tend to create a monopoly or monopsony. The term materially means more than a very small amount.

sections.title: Section 5 Post-Proceeding Data
sections.text: This section requires companies that settle antitrust cases involving mergers to provide annual reports for five years after the settlement. These reports must include information about pricing, availability, and quality of products or services covered by the agreement, any cost savings or benefits claimed from the merger and whether they were passed on to consumers, and how effective any required divestitures or conditions were in restoring competition. The requirement to provide this information must be included in settlement agreements. The Federal Trade Commission and Department of Justice will create rules about what information must be provided. A chief executive officer, chief financial officer, general counsel, or similar corporate officer must certify under penalty of perjury that the report is accurate.

sections.title: Section 6 Federal Trade Commission Study
sections.text: Within two years of this law being enacted, the Federal Trade Commission must conduct and publish a study about institutional investors owning competing companies in concentrated markets. The study will look at how much institutional investors own competitors, how this affects competition, and how institutional investors might influence competition among the companies they invest in. The study will use public data when available and include public comments. The Paperwork Reduction Act does not apply to collecting information for this study.

sections.title: Section 7 GAO Studies
sections.text: Within eighteen months of this law being enacted, the Government Accountability Office must conduct and publish a study to assess how successful merger remedies required by the Department of Justice or Federal Trade Commission have been over the past eight years. This includes looking at their impact on maintaining competition, comparing structural remedies (like requiring companies to sell parts of their business) with conduct remedies (like requiring certain behaviors), and whether divested assets remained viable. The GAO must also study how mergers and acquisitions affect wages, employment, innovation, and new business formation. The GAO must update the merger remedies study every four years and identify specific remedies or claimed merger benefits that need more research.

sections.title: Section 8 Office of Competition Advocate
sections.text: This section establishes an Office of Competition Advocate within the Federal Trade Commission. The Competition Advocate will report directly to the Chair but the Chair cannot prevent them from doing their duties. The Advocate is appointed by the Chair with approval of the Commission, including at least one Commissioner from a different political party, and serves a seven-year term. They can only be removed by unanimous vote of the Commission. The Advocate cannot have worked for the Commission in the two years before appointment and cannot work for the Commission for five years after leaving the position. The Commission must provide sufficient funding for the Advocate to hire necessary staff. The Competition Advocate will recommend processes for agencies to get reports about anticompetitive practices from consumers, small businesses, and workers, provide recommendations to other agencies about actions that may help or harm competition, publish periodic reports on the effects of remedies and enforcement actions, recommend improvements to antitrust enforcement guidelines and practices, report evidence of antitrust violations to the Commission or Attorney General, request information from any agency as needed, decide whether to release recommendations publicly, have access to all information collected by the Office of Market Analysis and Data, and submit all recommendations and reports to congressional judiciary committees. The Competition Advocate has independent authority to request voluntary reports from covered companies (companies that have filed under merger notification requirements) or compel such reports by subpoena. Before issuing a subpoena, the Advocate must find in writing that the data is needed and not available from public sources, voluntarily, or from other agencies. The Advocate must coordinate with other agencies when possible and has independent authority to enforce subpoenas in federal court. Information obtained is subject to the same confidentiality protections as information obtained by the Commission.

sections.title: Section 9 Office of Market Analysis and Data
sections.text: This section establishes an Office of Market Analysis and Data within the Federal Trade Commission. Working with the Bureau of Economics, this office will collect, validate, and maintain data from agencies, commercial data providers, public sources, covered companies, and data obtained through Commission authority. The office will prepare and publish databases on concentration and merger enforcement that are easily accessible to the public, collect and publish data on concentration levels across industries and antitrust enforcement, standardize data reporting formats, publish reports on competitive conditions affecting markets and industry sectors including effects on consumers, workers, innovation, economic competitiveness, and national security, and publish reports on the competitive effects of acquisitions including recommendations for enforcement action and assessments of market conditions since acquisitions occurred. These reports will examine impacts on prices including wages, output and quality, competitor entry or exit, innovation, consumer choice, supplier opportunities, coordination between competitors, and subsequent merger activity. They will also assess whether acquiring companies complied with settlement obligations and achieved claimed efficiencies, and whether settlement agreements or court remedies were effective in reducing anticompetitive effects. The Commission must keep data secure and may create regulations for data collection and standardization.

sections.title: Section 10 Exclusionary Conduct
sections.text: This section adds a new section 26A to the Clayton Act prohibiting exclusionary conduct. Exclusionary conduct means conduct that materially disadvantages actual or potential competitors or tends to foreclose or limit their ability or incentive to compete. However, applying for or enforcing patents, trademarks, or copyrights alone does not constitute exclusionary conduct unless done baselessly, in bad faith, or in violation of legal obligations. Similarly, conduct necessary to comply with federal or state law alone does not constitute exclusionary conduct. Both can be considered as part of a broader course of conduct. Market power is defined as the ability to profitably impose terms on others that are more favorable than what could be obtained in a competitive market. It is unlawful for a person, alone or with others, to engage in exclusionary conduct that presents an appreciable risk of harming competition. This also constitutes an unfair method of competition under the Federal Trade Commission Act. There is a presumption that exclusionary conduct violates the law if undertaken by a person or group with more than 50 percent market share or otherwise significant market power in a relevant market. This presumption can be overcome if the defendant proves by a preponderance of the evidence that distinct procompetitive benefits eliminate the risk of harm to competition, that other persons have entered or expanded in the market eliminating the risk of harm, or that the conduct does not present an appreciable risk of harming competition. If the presumption does not apply, the determination is based on the totality of circumstances, including whether procompetitive benefits substantially eliminate the risk of harm and whether others have entered or expanded substantially eliminating the risk. A violation does not require finding that the defendant altered prior dealings, treated people differently, engaged in below-cost pricing, will recoup losses from below-cost pricing, that conduct makes no economic sense apart from harming competition, that harm has been quantified, or for multi-sided platforms that conduct harms competition on more than one side. Civil penalties for violations can be up to the greater of 15 percent of total US revenues for the previous year or 30 percent of US revenues in affected commerce during the unlawful conduct period. The Federal Trade Commission can bring civil actions for these penalties and has exclusive authority unless it authorizes the Attorney General. Within one year, the Attorney General and Federal Trade Commission must issue joint guidelines on enforcement under this section, updating them at least every five years with 60 days public notice and comment.

sections.title: Section 11 Penalties for Sherman Act Violations
sections.text: This section adds civil penalty provisions to sections 1 and 2 of the Sherman Act and section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act. For Sherman Act violations, the civil or criminal penalty can be up to the greater of 15 percent of total US revenues for the previous year or 30 percent of US revenues in affected commerce during the unlawful conduct period. The penalty for section 1 violations can be recovered in civil or criminal actions, while section 2 penalties can only be recovered in civil actions. The Federal Trade Commission can bring civil actions for penalties under section 5 for unfair methods of competition that constitute Sherman Act violations, with the same penalty amounts. These civil penalties are in addition to, not instead of, other remedies available under federal law including the Clayton Act and Federal Trade Commission Act.

sections.title: Section 12 Joint Civil Penalty Guidelines
sections.text: Within one year of enactment, the Attorney General and Federal Trade Commission must issue joint guidelines for determining appropriate civil penalty amounts under the Sherman Act, Clayton Act section 26A, and Federal Trade Commission Act section 5. The goal is promoting transparency and seeking remedies that effectively deter future violations and are proportionate to the violation's gravity. Courts must consider the volume of commerce affected, duration and severity of conduct, intent, whether conduct was egregious or clearly violated the law, whether the penalty is combined with other remedies like structural relief or disgorgement, whether the person previously engaged in similar conduct, the penalty's deterrent effect, and whether conduct violated a preexisting consent decree or court order. The agencies must update guidelines at least every five years with 60 days public notice and comment. The Administrative Procedure Act's rulemaking provisions do not apply.

sections.title: Section 13 Market Definition
sections.text: Establishing liability under antitrust laws does not require defining a relevant market, except when market definition is required to establish a presumption or resolve a claim under a statutory provision that explicitly references relevant market, market concentration, or market share. References to line of commerce do not constitute an exception to this rule. If direct evidence in the record is sufficient to prove actual or likely harm to competition or an appreciable risk to competition, neither a court nor the Federal Trade Commission shall require market definition to evaluate evidence, find liability, or find that a claim has been stated. Nothing prevents a court or the Federal Trade Commission from considering evidence about proposed relevant markets to evaluate the merits of antitrust claims.

sections.title: Section 14 Limitations on Implied Immunity from the Antitrust Laws
sections.text: In any action to enforce antitrust laws regarding federally regulated conduct, no court may find that a federal statute implicitly prevents application of antitrust laws unless a federal agency actively regulates the conduct, the statute does not preserve antitrust rights or remedies, and agency rules explicitly require or authorize the defendant's conduct. In such actions, antitrust laws shall be applied fully without qualification or limitation, and their scope shall not be narrowed because of federal rules, regulations, or agencies, unless application is precluded by an explicit statutory exemption or an implied immunity satisfying the above requirements.

sections.title: Section 15 Whistleblower Protections
sections.text: This section adds section 27A to the Clayton Act protecting civil whistleblowers. Employers cannot discharge, demote, suspend, threaten, harass, or otherwise discriminate against covered individuals (employees, contractors, subcontractors, or agents) who provide information about antitrust violations to the federal government or supervisory personnel, or who participate in federal investigations or proceedings. Protections do not apply if the individual planned and initiated a violation of antitrust laws, criminal laws in conjunction with antitrust violations, or obstruction of federal investigations. Covered individuals can seek relief by filing complaints with the Secretary of Labor or, if no final decision is issued within 180 days, bringing court actions. Procedures follow those in title 49 section 42121. Complaints must be filed within 180 days of the violation. Remedies include reinstatement, back pay with interest, and compensation for special damages including litigation costs and attorney fees. The section also adds section 217 to the Antitrust Criminal Penalty Enhancement and Reform Act creating whistleblower rewards. The Attorney General may pay awards of 10 to 30 percent of collected criminal fines exceeding one million dollars to whistleblowers who voluntarily provide original information leading to successful enforcement. Original information must be derived from personal knowledge, not known to the government from other sources unless the whistleblower is the original source, not derived from public sources unless the whistleblower is the source, and not already required to be disclosed. Awards are denied to government employees, law enforcement personnel, those convicted of related crimes, originators or leaders of the violation, those who fail to provide timely truthful cooperation, those who commit crimes after disclosure, those who fail to submit required information, those who obstruct investigations, and those disqualified under the Leniency Program. Award determinations are at the Attorney General's discretion and may be appealed within 30 days except for amount determinations if the award was properly made.

sections.title: Section 16 Prejudgment Interest
sections.text: This section amends section 4 of the Clayton Act to provide that successful antitrust plaintiffs shall recover threefold damages, costs including reasonable attorney fees, and simple interest on the treble damages from the date of service of the pleading until judgment.

sections.title: Section 17 No Forced Arbitration for Antitrust Disputes
sections.text: This section adds chapter 5 to title 9 of the US Code prohibiting forced arbitration for antitrust disputes. An antitrust dispute is one arising from alleged violations of antitrust laws where plaintiffs seek class certification. A predispute arbitration agreement is an agreement to arbitrate disputes that have not yet arisen. A predispute joint-action waiver is an agreement prohibiting participation in joint, class, or collective actions for disputes that have not yet arisen. No predispute arbitration agreement or joint-action waiver is valid or enforceable for antitrust disputes. Courts, not arbitrators, determine whether this chapter applies and the validity of agreements, regardless of how the party challenges arbitration or whether the agreement delegates such determinations to an arbitrator. The section makes conforming amendments to other parts of title 9.

sections.title: Section 18 Additional Remedies; Rules of Construction
sections.text: The rights and remedies in this law are in addition to, not instead of, other rights and remedies under federal law including the Clayton Act and Federal Trade Commission Act. Nothing in this law impairs or limits the applicability of antitrust laws or prohibits other federal law remedies.

sections.title: Section 19 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: For fiscal year 2025, this section authorizes five hundred thirty-five million dollars for the Antitrust Division of the Department of Justice and seven hundred twenty-five million dollars for the Federal Trade Commission. Beginning in fiscal year 2026 and each year after, all premerger notification filing fees collected under the Clayton Act shall be retained and used for antitrust enforcement expenses by the Antitrust Division and Federal Trade Commission, remaining available until spent. These fees will be treated as direct spending under budget control laws.
